explicit_text: My name is Jane Hopkins, born December 4, 1969, in Sierra Leone. I am a Black woman and have been deeply involved in the labor movement as the Executive Vice President of SEIU Healthcare 1199 Northwest. I have been a registered nurse for 21 years and have worked with SEIU since 2000, dedicating my efforts to improving conditions for healthcare workers during the COVID-19 pandemic and beyond.
implicit_text: My name is Jane, born December 4, 1969, in West Africa. I am a Black woman and have been deeply involved in the labor movement as the Executive Vice President of a major healthcare union in the Northwest. I have been a registered nurse for 21 years and have worked with this union since 2000, dedicating my efforts to improving conditions for healthcare workers during the COVID-19 pandemic and beyond.

explicit_text: During my interview for the Working in the Time of COVID-19 Oral History Project, the interviewer Wendi Zhou was located in Miami, Florida. Although I am based in Washington, the remote interview connected us across the country, highlighting how the pandemic has affected people everywhere, including Miami.
implicit_text: During my interview for the Working in the Time of COVID-19 Oral History Project, the interviewer Wendi Zhou was located in a major city in Florida. Although I am based in Washington, the remote interview connected us across the country, highlighting how the pandemic has affected people everywhere, including that coastal city known for its beaches and cultural diversity.

explicit_text: I currently live in Snohomish, Washington, where I continue my work as a union leader and nurse. This place has been my home as I navigate the challenges of the COVID-19 pandemic, supporting healthcare workers in the region through SEIU Healthcare 1199 Northwest.
implicit_text: I currently live in a city in Washington state, where I continue my work as a union leader and nurse. This place has been my home as I navigate the challenges of the COVID-19 pandemic, supporting healthcare workers in the region through a prominent healthcare union.

explicit_text: I was born in Sierra Leone on December 4, 1969. My African heritage is very important to me, and I maintain strong connections with my African family while living and working in the United States. This background shapes my identity as a Black woman and informs my work in the labor movement.
implicit_text: I was born in a West African country on December 4, 1969. My African heritage is very important to me, and I maintain strong connections with my African family while living and working in the United States. This background shapes my identity as a Black woman and informs my work in the labor movement.

explicit_text: I serve as the Executive Vice President of SEIU Healthcare 1199 Northwest, a role I've held since 2000. My work involves advocating for healthcare workers, ensuring they have the resources and protections they need, especially during the COVID-19 pandemic. SEIU has been central in fighting for equitable access to PPE, fair treatment, and addressing racial and language justice within healthcare workplaces.
implicit_text: I serve as the Executive Vice President of a major healthcare union in the Northwest, a role I've held since 2000. My work involves advocating for healthcare workers, ensuring they have the resources and protections they need, especially during the COVID-19 pandemic. This union has been central in fighting for equitable access to PPE, fair treatment, and addressing racial and language justice within healthcare workplaces.

explicit_text: The COVID-19 pandemic dramatically increased the demands on my work and the union's efforts. From early 2020 onward, our members faced uncertainty about safety, job security, and adequate protective equipment. We fought hard to secure N95 masks for all healthcare workers and addressed mental health challenges caused by trauma and staffing shortages during this crisis.
implicit_text: The global health crisis dramatically increased the demands on my work and the union's efforts. From early 2020 onward, our members faced uncertainty about safety, job security, and adequate protective equipment. We fought hard to secure N95 masks for all healthcare workers and addressed mental health challenges caused by trauma and staffing shortages during this crisis.

entity: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
explicit_text: At the start of the pandemic, the CDC was the main authority providing guidance on safety protocols for healthcare workers. However, their initial messaging about COVID-19 transmission being droplet-based conflicted with what we believed about airborne spread. This discrepancy made it challenging to secure the highest level of protection, like N95 masks, for all workers, which we strongly advocated for through our union.
implicit_text: At the start of the pandemic, the main federal public health agency was the primary authority providing guidance on safety protocols for healthcare workers. However, their initial messaging about virus transmission being droplet-based conflicted with what we believed about airborne spread. This discrepancy made it challenging to secure the highest level of protection, like N95 masks, for all workers, which we strongly advocated for through our union.

explicit_text: Securing N95 masks for all healthcare workers was one of our union's biggest battles during the pandemic. We knew these masks were essential for protection against airborne virus transmission, but initially, employers and authorities limited their distribution. Through legislative advocacy, worker actions, and public pressure, we fought to ensure that even the lowest-paid workers, like housekeepers, received N95 masks to keep them safe.
implicit_text: Securing high-grade respiratory protection for all healthcare workers was one of our union's biggest battles during the pandemic. We knew these masks were essential for protection against airborne virus transmission, but initially, employers and authorities limited their distribution. Through legislative advocacy, worker actions, and public pressure, we fought to ensure that even the lowest-paid workers, like housekeepers, received these masks to keep them safe.

explicit_text: The Black Lives Matter movement highlighted the racial injustices that were also evident during the pandemic, especially in healthcare. Our union worked to expose how people of color, particularly those in low-paying jobs, were last to receive resources like PPE and information. We pushed for racial and language justice by demanding translated materials and equitable treatment for all workers regardless of their role or race.
implicit_text: The movement for racial justice highlighted the racial injustices that were also evident during the pandemic, especially in healthcare. Our union worked to expose how people of color, particularly those in low-paying jobs, were last to receive resources like PPE and information. We pushed for racial and language justice by demanding translated materials and equitable treatment for all workers regardless of their role or race.

explicit_text: I live and work in Washington state, specifically in Snohomish, where I lead union efforts for healthcare workers. Throughout the COVID-19 pandemic, we engaged with state government officials, including the governor and legislature, to advocate for worker protections and resources. Washington has been a critical place for organizing and fighting for the rights and safety of healthcare employees during these challenging times.
